matched.risk:
Introduces an enumeration whose semantics are ambiguous: "all of
these are required" (AND) vs "any of these qualifies" (OR). A
bundle authored without disambiguation often encodes the
conservative AND reading. Many real-world labelling conventions
use OR (the "defining feature" view), and a clean canonical-source
authoring run can score sharply lower than the labelled answer
key in that case.
matched.suggested_hint_template:
In real-world practice, [the X prong] is the defining feature of
[the rule]. [Other prongs] are typical companion controls but
not strictly required. A clause containing only [the X prong]
should still be classified as [the rule].

synonyms: subsidiary that is not a Restricted Subsidiary
subsidiary that is not a Guarantor
synonym_families.note:
Standard LSTA Model Credit Agreement terminology. Drafting
practice varies; all of the above refer to subsidiaries outside
the loan's restricted/guaranteed group. A prohibition or
restriction targeting any of these terms is a prohibition on
transfer to an unrestricted subsidiary for J.Crew Blocker
identification.

seen_in.observation:
The single remaining miss after rule-authoring SME hints
(1/54 in the v3 bundle) was a clause using "non-Loan Party
Subsidiary" terminology. Adding this synonym family to the
extractor prompt resolved it; v4 scored 54/54 (100%).

synonyms: intellectual property material to the business of the Borrower
synonym_families.note:
Real J.Crew Blocker provisions almost always restrict only
"material IP" rather than "all intellectual property". This
narrower scope is consistent with the prohibition prong; the
original 2016 J.Crew workaround targeted material IP
specifically. Should be treated as equivalent to the canonical
term for prong-detection purposes.

synonyms: subsidiary that is a Loan Party
synonym_families.note:
The complement of the unrestricted-subsidiary class. Frequently
used in carve-outs ("transfers between Restricted Subsidiaries
are permitted"). Important for the assessor to recognise so it
doesn't falsely flag carve-out language as a prohibition.

suggested_hints.template:
In real-world practice, [the X prong] is the defining feature of
[the rule]. [Other prongs] are typical companion controls but
not strictly required. A clause containing only [the X prong]
should still be classified as [the rule].
suggested_hints.rationale: Source contains markers triggered by `typically-includes` (severity: medium). Adding a rule-authoring hint that disambiguates the AND/OR reading materially changes how the bundle encodes the rule.

suggested_hints.template:
Recognise the following synonym families when reading case narratives — all members of a family refer to the same underlying concept for prong-detection purposes:
  - "unrestricted subsidiary" ≡ "non-Loan Party Subsidiary", "non-Restricted Subsidiary", "non-Guarantor Subsidiary", "Excluded Subsidiary", "subsidiary that is not a Restricted Subsidiary", "subsidiary that is not a Guarantor"
  - "material intellectual property" ≡ "Material Intellectual Property", "material IP", "Material IP", "intellectual property material to the business of the Borrower"
  - "restricted subsidiary" ≡ "Restricted Subsidiary", "Loan Party Subsidiary", "Guarantor Subsidiary", "subsidiary that is a Loan Party"
suggested_hints.rationale: Real drafting practice uses synonyms for canonical terms. The runtime LLM extractor needs domain-specific equivalences or it will miss the prong on cases that use alternative terminology.
